title: Thailand Temples — Must-Visit Sacred Sites
meta_description: Guide to 16 must-visit temples across Thailand with entry fees, opening hours and key facts. From Bangkok's Wat Pho to Chiang Rai's White Temple.
intro: After visiting dozens of temples across Thailand over multiple trips, these 16 stand out as the ones that left the deepest impression. Each has its own character — from the ancient Khmer stonework of Phanom Rung to the contemporary all-white vision of Wat Rong Khun. I've included real entry fees, opening hours where available, and the practical details you need to plan your visits. Thai temples are active places of worship, so approach them with the same respect you'd bring to any sacred site.

items.name: Wat Pho (Temple of the Reclining Buddha)
items.description: Home to Thailand's largest reclining Buddha at 46 metres long and 15 metres high, Wat Pho also holds the country's largest collection of Buddha images. The temple is recognised as Thailand's first university, historically a centre for education in medicine and Thai massage.
key_facts: Largest collection of Buddha images in Thailand

items.name: Wat Arun (Temple of Dawn)
items.description: Rising over 70 metres along the Chao Phraya River, Wat Arun's spires are covered in colourful porcelain and glass fragments that catch the light throughout the day. Known as the Temple of Dawn, it is one of Bangkok's most recognisable landmarks.

items.name: Wat Saket (Golden Mount)
items.description: Built on an 80-metre artificial hill, Wat Saket requires a climb of 300 steps to reach the golden chedi at the top. The reward is a full 360-degree panoramic view across Bangkok's skyline.
items.google_maps_query: Wat Saket Golden Mount Bangkok Thailand

items.name: Wat Phra Kaew (Temple of the Emerald Buddha)
items.description: Located within the Grand Palace complex, Wat Phra Kaew houses the Emerald Buddha — Thailand's most sacred Buddhist image. Entry is included with the Grand Palace admission fee.

items.name: Wat Phra That Doi Suthep
items.description: Perched on a mountainside above Chiang Mai, this temple is reached via a 306-step Naga stairway flanked by serpent balustrades. Built in 1383, the golden pagoda is said to contain a relic of the Buddha.
items.google_maps_query: Wat Phra That Doi Suthep Chiang Mai Thailand

items.name: Wat Chedi Luang
items.description: Dating from the 14th century, Wat Chedi Luang features a massive stupa adorned with elephant statues. The temple is a prime example of Lanna architecture and remains one of Chiang Mai's most important religious sites.
items.google_maps_query: Wat Chedi Luang Chiang Mai Thailand

items.name: Wat Suan Dok
items.description: The 'Flower Garden Temple' dates from the 14th century and is known for its 48-metre golden chedi surrounded by a collection of white pagodas containing royal ashes. The expansive grounds offer a peaceful atmosphere.
items.google_maps_query: Wat Suan Dok Chiang Mai Thailand

items.name: Wat U Mong
items.description: A 14th-century forest temple set among trees on the outskirts of Chiang Mai. Wat U Mong is distinctive for its underground tunnels and shrines, giving it a meditative atmosphere unlike any other temple in the region.
items.google_maps_query: Wat U Mong Chiang Mai Thailand

items.name: Wat Rong Khun (White Temple)
items.description: A contemporary art exhibit in the form of a Buddhist temple, Wat Rong Khun is entirely white and covered in mirrored glass. This privately owned creation blends traditional Thai temple elements with modern artistic expression.
items.google_maps_query: Wat Rong Khun White Temple Chiang Rai Thailand

items.name: Wat Rong Seur Ten (Blue Temple)
items.description: Unlike the nearby White Temple, the Blue Temple is an actual functioning Buddhist temple. Its striking all-blue interior and exterior make it one of the most visually distinctive temples in Thailand.
items.google_maps_query: Wat Rong Seur Ten Blue Temple Chiang Rai Thailand

items.name: Wat Phra Sri Sanphet
items.description: The most important temple in the ancient city of Ayutthaya, Wat Phra Sri Sanphet stands among the ruins of the former capital. Its three iconic chedis are the enduring symbol of Ayutthaya's historical grandeur.
items.google_maps_query: Wat Phra Sri Sanphet Ayutthaya Thailand

items.name: Tiger Cave Temple (Wat Tham Suea)
items.description: A challenging climb of 1,260 steps to an elevation of 278 metres rewards visitors with sweeping views over Krabi province. The temple complex at the base includes caves and shrines set into the limestone karst landscape.
items.google_maps_query: Tiger Cave Temple Wat Tham Suea Krabi Thailand

items.name: Big Buddha (Wat Phra Yai)
items.description: The 45-metre tall white marble Buddha sits atop Nakkerd Hill, visible from much of the island. The hilltop location provides 360-degree views across Phuket's coastline and interior.
items.google_maps_query: Big Buddha Wat Phra Yai Phuket Thailand

items.name: Phanom Rung Historical Park
items.description: A Khmer temple complex built from laterite and sandstone, Phanom Rung resembles the architecture of Angkor Wat in Cambodia. Situated on an extinct volcano, it is one of the finest examples of Khmer architecture in Thailand.
items.google_maps_query: Phanom Rung Historical Park Buriram Thailand

items.name: Sanctuary of Truth
items.description: An ongoing construction project and artistic masterpiece, the Sanctuary of Truth is built entirely from hand-carved wood without the use of nails. The structure sits on the oceanfront in Pattaya and blends religious and philosophical themes from across Asia.

items.name: Wat Phra That Mae Yen
items.description: A hilltop temple east of Pai town, reached by climbing 353 steps. The giant white Buddha statue at the top overlooks the Pai valley and surrounding mountains.
items.google_maps_query: Wat Phra That Mae Yen Pai Thailand

tips: Remove shoes before entering temple buildings — look for shoes at the entrance as a cue
Cover shoulders and knees; many temples provide wraps if needed but bringing your own is more reliable
Never point your feet at a Buddha image or monk
Speak quietly and turn off phone sounds inside temple halls
Women should not touch or hand objects directly to monks
Avoid climbing on Buddha statues or ruins for photos
Bring water and sun protection — many temples involve outdoor walking with limited shade
Visit early morning for cooler temperatures, fewer crowds, and the chance to see monks' morning rituals
